summary_one_line: A parsed PI-target name whose first three chars are x/X,m/M,l/L (e.g. literal xMl inside <?xMl) to take name[1]=='m'/'M' true

hypotheses.what_input_feature: An XML processing instruction whose target name parses to a 3+ char token beginning x/X then m/M then l/L, so that name[1] is 'm' or 'M'; the winner needs the literal byte run '<?xMl' (or '<?xml') where the second name char is m/M, not an arbitrary perturbation like '<?x_l'.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: The naive winner-resolving seed carries the literal bytes '<?xMl version=' at offsets 0x16-0x1d ('3c(<) 3f(?) 78(x) 4d(M) 6c(l) 20( )'), so xmlParsePITarget sees name[0]=='x',name[1]=='M',name[2]=='l' and the line 5129 true side is taken; W shows the blocker true side hit (T=3 F=0).
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: naive_ctx loser seeds carry corrupted PI targets ('<?x_l' with byte 5f('_') at the name[1] position, plus xmltp/xsim variants), so name[1] is not m/M and line 5129 only ever takes the false side; the per-role overlay records the blocker exclusively as L false (T=0 F=12).
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: naive (the winner) uses a bare per-edge MaxMapFeedback, so once havoc/token mutation lands an 'x?l'->'xMl' byte that flips line 5128 to true the new edge at 5129 is immediately rewarded. naive_ctx (the loser) folds a call-context hash into the edge-map index (CtxHook), so the same parser edges are split across many (call-context, edge) slots; the feedback signal that should guide mutation toward the xMl name is diluted across contexts, the corpus fills with context-duplicate inputs that never carry a valid xml-prefixed PI target, and the branch stays blocked in all 10 trials.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocker is the name[1]=='m'/'M' check at parser.c:5129, true side globally blocked.
The decisive pair is naive beating naive_ctx with delta ctx_coverage; naive_ctx is the loser blocked in all 10 trials.
The winner resolved 8/10 while naive_ctx resolved 0/10 and stayed blocked 10/10.
The winner-resolving seed contains the literal '<?xMl version=' byte run carrying x,M,l as the name characters.
A loser-blocking seed has a corrupted PI target '<?x_l' where the name[1] position is '_' (0x5f) rather than m/M.
On the winner the blocker true side is taken (T=3 F=0) while on the loser it is only ever false (T=0 F=12).
naive's feedback is a bare per-edge hit-count bucket with no context indexing.

evidence_trail.claim: naive_ctx treats a new (call-context, edge) pair as new coverage rather than a bare edge, which fragments the signal.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: claimed_mechanism is context-sensitive coverage (CtxHook), not I2SRandReplace, so the mandatory I2S lineage query does not apply. The mechanism here is a feedback-granularity disadvantage of the loser naive_ctx, not an I2S substitution by the winner; the winner naive uses only havoc/token mutation (MECHANISM CONTEXT: 'No I2S'). No dash-row lineage check is required for a non-I2S attribution.

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: A synthetic harness with the same x/m/l three-char PI-target gate where naive_ctx resolves the branch as fast as or faster than naive across trials, showing the context-indexed edge map does not dilute the feedback that guides mutation toward the literal name bytes.

weakest_evidence_point: The attribution that context-hash fragmentation (not mere run-to-run variance over a single 3-byte gate) is what blocks naive_ctx in 10/10 trials rests on mechanism reasoning rather than direct evidence of corpus context-duplication.
